items.q: What does the M+ peak in a mass spectrum represent?
items.correct: The molecular ion — formed when a single electron is removed from the whole molecule, giving the relative molecular mass.
items.misconception: The M+ peak shows the mass of the largest fragment after the molecule breaks apart.
items.random_wrong: The M+ peak shows the mass of a single hydrogen atom added to the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse the M+ peak (the parent ion at the molecular mass) with fragment peaks; they think the highest mass peak is always a fragment, not the intact ionised molecule.

items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: Why does water have a bent shape rather than linear?
items.correct: Oxygen has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral electron geometry but a bent molecular shape.
items.misconception: Water is bent because the hydrogens repel each other and push apart.
items.random_wrong: Water is bent because oxygen is heavier than hydrogen and weighs the middle down.
items.why: Students see H atoms at the ends and intuit H-H repulsion, missing that the bend is caused by lone pair-bond pair repulsion on the oxygen.
It's Polyamide. “Polyester” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Diol and dicarboxylic acid. “Two amines” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
